of electron densities in the ISM and the CGM. This largely
unexplored gap motivates us to investigate the variation of
ne in the spatially as well as spectroscopically well-resolved
galaxies in the SDSS Mapping Nearby Galaxies at Apache
Point Observatory (MaNGA) data.
For our own galaxy, the NE2001 (Cordes & Lazio (2002))
and YMW16 (Yao et al. (2017)) models have used disper-
sion measures (DM) from pulsars in the Milky Way and the
Magellanic Clouds, as well as extragalactic sources, includ-
ing Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs), to estimate the electron den-
sities and build its profile. Similarly, the DMs of the FRBs
offer a means to probe the electron densities of their host
galaxies and any intervening galaxies (Petroff et al. (2019)).
The observed DM is influenced by the contributions from the
Milky Way and the intergalactic medium (IGM), necessitat-
ing accurate models of these components to segregate the
host contribution. Nevertheless, these observations provide
electron density estimates only at specific radii given by the
line of sight, and cannot give the complete picture of the spa-
tial variation of the ne in the host. We, therefore, look into
other probes.

To investigate the extent and structure of the electron den-
sity in the galaxies, we have used data releases from the
IFS surveys. In several past studies Cappellari et al. (2011);
Croom et al. (2012); Sa´nchez, S. F. et al. (2012); Brodie et al.
(2014); Ma et al. (2014), authors have used different IFU sur-
3D
veys ATLAS  , SAMI, SLUGGS, MASSIVE, and CALIFA
1
, having different characteristics and source properties.  In
this study, we have used the data from the SDSS MaNGA
survey, which is the largest existing IFS survey with ∼10000
nearby galaxies. The MaNGA survey has been chosen for
two main reasons:  (i) it provides a continuous coverage of
wavelength that allows us full range model fitting for many
spectral features, (ii) the spectral resolution of MaNGA is
R ∼ 2000 at the average redshift (⟨z⟩ ∼ 0.03).
2.1. The MaNGA survey
The MaNGA survey is one of the three major programs of
the fourth-generation SDSS-IV (Bundy et al. (2015)). It uses
the 2.5m Sloan Foundation Telescope (Gunn et al. (2006))
at Apache Point Observatory (APO) to observe a sample
∼ 10000 galaxies comprised of all morphological types. The
targets cover an almost flat distribution in stellar mass (M∗)
9  11
with a range of 10 M⊙ < M∗ < 10 M⊙, and a red-
shift interval of 0.01 ≤ z ≤ 0.15 (Blanton et al. (2017)).
MaNGA uses the IFU spectroscopy technique, observing
each galaxy with 19-127 hexagonal fiber bundles,  corre-
sponding to a diameter between 12-32 arcsec in the sky. The
two dual-channel Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey
(BOSS) spectrographs (Smee et al. (2013)) provide simulta-
neous spectra that have a wavelength coverage range of 3600-
10300 A˚  with a median spectral resolution of R ∼ 2000.
More details on the observing strategy, calibration, and sur-
vey design of MaNGA can be found in Law et al. (2015), Yan
et al. (2015), and Wake et al. (2017).
Two-thirds (∼5000) of the MaNGA galaxies have a field
of view covering 1.5 times the effective radius (Re; which
contains half of the galaxy light  in r-band) and one-third
(∼3300) covers out to 2.5Re and more. These are referred
to as the primary and secondary samples, respectively.  A
Colour-Enhanced sample of an additional ∼1700 galaxies
are added to the primary sample to balance the color distri-
bution at fixed M∗. Hence, this collective sample is known
as “primary+” sample. The primary+ sample has an average
redshift of ⟨z⟩ ∼ 0.03, while the secondary sample has a
higher average redshift of ⟨z⟩ ∼ 0.045.
We have utilised the data products of the MaNGA Data
Analysis Pipeline (DAP; Westfall et al. (2019), Belfiore et al.
(2019)) and pyPipe3D pipeline (hereafter Pipe3D) (Lac-
erda et al. (2022) and Sa´nchez et al. (2022)).  In both ap-
proaches,  the stellar continuum is subtracted from the ob-

2.2. Sample selection
In order to obtain the radial profile of electron density,
we require the emission-line fluxes of certain spectral lines.
Since the observed line fluxes represent the integrated emis-
sion along the line of sight, a face-on orientation minimizes
projection effects and internal dust extinction, thereby allow-
ing a better interpretation of the radial distribution (Zhang
et al. (2016) and Poetrodjojo et al. (2018)). Thus, we focus
only on the face-on galaxies for our analysis.

3.1. Electron Density Diagnostics
The electron density in a gas cloud or an H II region can
be determined by analysing the effects of collisional exci-
tation and de-excitation.  This is achieved by comparing
the intensities of two lines from the same ion originating
from closely lying energy levels. Since the excitation rates
to these levels are primarily governed by the ratio of their
collision strengths, differences in their radiative transition
probabilities or collisional de-excitation rates cause the rel-
ative populations of the levels to vary with density (see Ap-
pendix A). Consequently,  the ratio of the emitted line in-
tensities becomes a reliable diagnostic for determining the
electron density (Osterbrock & Ferland (2006), Dopita &
Sutherland (2013)). There are several doublets that fall in this
regime and lie in a wide optical range, such as [O II] λλ3726,
3729 A˚ , [S II] λλ6716, 6731 A˚ , [Ar IV] λλ4711, 4740 A˚ and
[Cl III] λλ5517, 5537 A˚  (Kewley et al. (2019)).The [Ar IV]
doublet is a high excitation state and hence sensitive to tem-
perature. Also, the Ar λ4711 A˚ line requires very high spec-
tral resolution as it gets blended with the He λ4713 A˚  line.
Along with ne, the [Cl III] line ratio is also sensitive to high
pressure and metallicities (< 0.1 dex of change in ne re-
sults in 0.2 dex change in metallicity). Therefore, we have
avoided these two doublets for our analysis. On the other
hand, the singly ionized species [S II] and [O II] have low ion-

ization energy and the closely lying levels result in transitions
that are weakly dependent on temperature and hence, these
have been widely used extensively in literature. Therefore in
our analysis, we use the commonly used diagnostic doublets:
[S II] λλ6716, 6731 A˚ , hereafter referred to as s1 (6716) and
s2 (6731), and [O II] λλ3726, 3729 A˚ , hereafter referred to
as o1 (3726) and o2 (3729), to estimate the electron densities.
We adopt the method from Sanders et al. (2015) to derive the
electron density using the line ratios described as,

S II, and 0.3771, 2468 and 638.4 for O II following Sanders
et al. (2015). These values are based on the latest atomic data
for transition probabilities from Fischer & Tachiev (2014)

3.2. Methodology
3.2.1. Flux analysis
In this analysis, we have used the emission line fluxes from
the Pipe3D and MaNGA DAP. Both pipelines provide the
line fluxes obtained by fitting a Gaussian to the emission lines
and using weighted moment analysis. In this work, we have
utilised the Gaussian-fitted fluxes from DAP and moment
analysis fluxes from Pipe3D pipeline.
Pipe3D analysis:  The S II  and O II  lines fluxes  for
each galaxy have been taken from the data products ob-
tained from Sa´nchez et al. (2022) provided in the extension
“FLUX ELINES LONG”. We begin the analysis by plotting
the spatially resolved maps of the fluxes s1, s2, o1 and o2.
We find that the outer regions of some of the galaxies show
certain pixels having negative flux values, which arise due to
incorrect sky calibration and subtraction.

For each galaxy, we

As a consequence, the number of accepted bins in S II and

to remove 9 Non-SFGs since the spatial coverage in the re-
maining bins of these galaxies was not significant enough to
carry out further analysis. Hence, this reduces our sample to
13 galaxies with 9 SFGs and 4 Non-SFGs. In the 13 galaxies,
the masked pixels were interpolated in the resulting maps us-
ing bilinear interpolation. We have used the convolution and

interpolation functions from Astropy , where these methods
are well implemented.
The flux maps were translated to the flux ratios RS II and
RO II . As discussed in subsection 3.1,  the dependencies of
electron density on R are sensitive to a range of R val-
ues.  Therefore, we choose the RS II and RO II values to lie
within the range of (0.4315, 1.449] (excluding 0.4315) and
(0.3771, 1.457] (excluding 0.3771), respectively, which re-

4
butions using the interquartile range (IQR)  outlier removal
technique.  The clipped pixels having RS II ≤ 0.4315 (and

By using Eq. 3, we calculate the electron densities, ne(S II)
and ne(O II) from RS II and RO II , respectively.
DAP analysis:  In this case, we followed a similar pro-

cussed below. At first, we used the Gaussian fitted emission
lines fluxes of S II and O II doublets, provided in the “EM-
LINE GFLUX” extension of the datacubes. We have used
the mask provided in the data cubes to remove the negative

IQR := Q3 − Q1 where Q1 and Q3 are the first and the third quantiles,
respectively. The data points below the lower, L = Q1 − 1.5 · IQR, and
above the upper, U = Q3 + 1.5 · IQR, thresholds are rejected as outliers.
This method is very robust for skewed distributions.

the outermost bin must have at least one-pixel radial separa-
tion). Among each bin, the weighted average of the electron
densities has been calculated as,

Fig. 2 and 3 illustrate the radial distributions of the elec-
tron density for each galaxy, employing linear and non-linear
binning, respectively, obtained by using the Pipe3D data.
Each data point represents the weighted average of the elec-
tron density calculated using Eq. 4 in different bins of each
galaxy and colour-coded accordingly. The colour panels on
the right show the MaNGA-ID of SFGs and Non-SFGs in
the upper and lower panels, respectively, arranged in the de-
creasing order of sSFR. The left and right panels represent
the density scatter plot obtained from S II and O II, respec-
tively, for SFGs and the lower panels represent the same for
the Non-SFGs. We find that the individual galaxy profiles do
not differ much when comparing the two binning schemes.
Also, we observe that the scatter plots for both SFGs and
Non-SFGs exhibit a qualitative decreasing trend in the case
of both S II and O II. To quantify the effect statistically, we
distribute the data points into bins of radial distance r/Re.
Since the MaNGA sample is not volume-limited, any statisti-
cal analysis requires a volume correction for all the galaxies,

′
subsamples, we adopt the volume weights (w ) calculated by

discussed in Section 6.4 of Wake et al. (2017), the inclina-
tion selection can introduce a bias due to higher extinction in
edge-on galaxies than in face-on galaxies.

Since our sample
purely consists of face-on galaxies, there will be no such bias
in our analysis and the standard volume weights can be used.
Thus, we calculate the weighted average of electron densities
in each radial bin as,

Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 are described in the same manner as Fig. 2
and Fig. 3. The obtained profiles have been discussed in de-

3.2.3. Electron Column Density
Noting that the observed line emission is always an in-
tegrated contribution along the line of sight, the more use-
ful physical measure is the electron column density, Ne :=
(cid:82)
ne dl, a line integral through the length of the medium. The
absorption line studies have been used as a successful tool to
obtain the gas column densities (see Werk et al. (2014) and
Tumlinson et al. (2017)). Translating the ne to Ne also de-
pends on the galactic disk model to be considered. We as-
sume an evenly distributed thin disk of a constant thickness
of 1 kpc for the region outside the central bulge.
To obtain the column density profiles, we have translated
the profiles from Fig 4 and 7 using 1 kpc disk thickness. The
profiles are depicted in Fig. 8 and will be discussed shortly.

provided in Table 2. The upper left panel depict the ne(S II)
profiles for SFGs. The red dotted and black dashed lines rep-

and the same convention has been followed throughout this
paper. We find that the electron density decreases with in-
creasing radial distance. The average density at r/Re ≤ 1.5

In the case of DAP, we have employed a similar procedure
that was implemented in the Pipe3D case to obtain the ra-
dial profiles from the scatter plots (Fig. 5 and Fig. 6). Fig. 7
illustrates the variation of ⟨new′ ⟩ with r/Re. For SFGs, in the
upper panels, we observe decreasing trends of ne(S II) and
ne(O II) with increasing radial distance. The ne(S II) aver-

above the demarcation are 112 ± 13.6 cm  and 60.3 ± 7.3

cm  , respectively, for linear binning and 93.8 ± 11.8 cm
−3
and 47±8.2 cm  , respectively, for non-linear binning. It is
interesting to note that in SFGs, both Pipe3D and DAP anal-
ysis yield radial profiles that exhibit similar trends. However,
for Non-SFGs, distinct variations are observed. The lower
left panel illustrates the radial variation of ne(S II), where the
−3
electron density gets falttend at around ∼33 cm  for linear.

Over the past two decades, advancements in high spectral
resolution instruments have made the study of electron den-
sity in galaxies increasingly prevalent. In the existing litera-
ture, the [S II] and [O II] doublets are the most common tools
that have been used to estimate the electron densities. There
have been several studies, for example, Sanders et al. (2015);
Kaasinen et al. (2016), Davies et al. (2021), Herrera-Camus
et al. (2016) and Isobe et al. (2023) have probed ne at dif-
ferent redshift using various lines and surveys. While these

11

ty profiles for SFGs obtained using Pipe3D data. The left and right
from the corresponding ne profiles in Fig. 4. The linear and non-linear
r panel: Similarly, the lower panels illustrate the corresponding radial
ts appear close to the horizontal axis; however, these represent column
zero.
studies offer valuable insights into the redshift evolution of
electron density, they primarily rely on integrated measure-
ments that average over the entire galaxy,  thereby lacking
information on spatial variations in the galactic disk. This
limitation becomes particularly important in the context of
magnetic field studies that utilise Faraday Rotation, where
the observed RM is influenced by the local electron density
at the specific impact parameter along the quasar sightline.
Therefore, the distribution of ne within the ISM and CGM of
galaxies remains questionable, which we have addressed in
this work.
We have presented the radial profiles of thermal electron
densities in the ISM of nearby face-on galaxies (zmed ∼
0.053).  The electron densities are calculated using colli-
sionally excited/de-excited [S II] λλ6716, 6731 A˚  and [O II]
λλ3726, 3729 A˚ doublets (Kewley et al. (2019)). We have
categorised our sample into SFGs and Non-SFGs based on
sSFR. The analysis was done using data from two pipelines:
Pipe3D and MaNGA DAP, including two different binning

In this study, we have investigated the radial distribution
of thermal electron densities in the disks of nearby face-on
galaxies using the emission line flux from IFU observations
from the SDSS MaNGA survey.
1. We estimated the electron densities and its radial vari-
ations for the 13 face-on galaxies (9 SFGs and 4 Non-
SFGs) by employing the collisionally originated emis-
sion doublet  lines of S II and O II fluxes from two
MaNGA pipelines Pipe3D and MaNGA DAP. Af-
ter considering volume-weights of each galaxy, we de-
rived an average radial profile, which shows a decreas-
ing trend.
2. In the case of Pipe3D, for SFGs we find that both
ne(S II) and ne(O II) are 165.6±20.8 and 354.4±13.3
−3
cm  ,  respectively in the inner disk  (r/Re ≤ 1.5),
which decrease on going towards the outer disk (r/Re
−3
> 1.5), to 31±4.5 and 302.1±10.6 cm  . These radial
gradients are also observed in Non-SFGs and remain
consistent across both S II and O II diagnostics.
3. For SFGs in the DAP data, both ne(S II) and ne(O II)
show a decreasing profile, with the estimates being
−3
74.3±13 and 112±13.6 cm  in the inner disk (r/Re
−3
≤1.5 ) to 12.1 ± 3.1 and 60.3 ± 7.3 cm  in the outer
disk (r/Re >1.5), respectively. Non-SFGs show a non-
monotonic profile that could arise due to small sample
size.
